learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate an understanding of how social policy is developed across community and government.
Demonstrate knowledge of policy concepts and design principles.
Analyse social policy through ideological and evidence informed perspectives.
Articulate the differences and similarities between Australia's welfare state and the development of welfare states in other western countries.
Critique representations of social policy issues and competing arguments.

assessments.task_description:
Exam content:

  • The exam will assess the content covered in Weeks 1-3 of the course. Both the required readings and lecture materials are assessable.


Exam structure:

  • The exam is made up of two (2) parts:
  • Part A: 10 x multiple choice questions worth 1 mark each (total of 10 marks available, weighted at 10% of your final mark)
  • Part B: 6 x short answer questions worth 5 marks each (total of 30 marks available, weighted at 15% of your final mark)
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.assessment_title: Analysing social policy using WPR approach
assessments.task_description:
This description is only a summary of the task. For full details, including the marking rubric, please refer to the course Learn UQ (Blackboard) site

Policies are often thought about as solutions to ‘problems’. Bacchi’s (2009) ‘What’s the problem represented to be?’ (WPR) approach challenges this idea. It suggests that policy solutions actually create or give shape to ‘problems’ by representing them in particular ways. When we analyse a solution that is offered to ‘fix’ a ‘problem’, we can learn something about how the problem is being thought about, that is, being represented. The WPR approach outlines a series of questions that can be used as a framework to analyse policy solutions and uncover what the problem is represented to be within them.

This task requires you to explain Bacchi’s (2009) WPR approach and then apply it to analyse social policy. Specifically, you will use WPR questions 1, 2 and 4 to analyse the policy statement of an Australian political party on a particular social policy issue.

The topic options for this assignment will become available on UQ. Learn (Blackboard) in Week 3.
assessments.submission_guidelines: All courses require students to submit an electronic version of their assignment via Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Analysing the impact of social policy
assessments.category: Essay/ Critique, Participation/ Student contribution, Presentation, Poster
assessments.task_description:
Assessment 3 Task Description

This description is only a summary of the task. For full details, including the marking rubric, please refer to the course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) site.

McClelland (2021) suggests that “social policy aims to improve people's well-being and is especially concerned with the welfare of those who experience some form of disadvantage” (p.3). This assignment requires you to analyse a social policy or program in order to explore whether the policy/program is meeting the needs of its intended target group. This task involves two parts.

Part A: Individual Policy/Program Evaluation Report (1,775 words)

You will select one (1) of the social policies/programs that will be made available on Blackboard in Week 8. This policy/program will be the focus of your written evaluation report.

To successfully complete Part A, you will need to access evaluations and academic sources that examine how your chosen policy/program is working in practice. Your report should analyse:

  • how the policy/program was developed across community and government
  • the interface between politics and policy
  • the historical, economic, ideological and evidence‑based perspectives shaping the issue
  • the contested concepts and principles underpinning the policy/program
  • the impacts on the target group, including issues of inequality and disadvantage
  • competing arguments and representations of the policy issue

This report forms the analytical foundation for Part B.

Part B: Social Policy Conference Presentation

In Weeks 12–13, you will participate in a live Social Policy Conference during your allocated tutorial time. Based on the topic you have chosen, you will be allocated to a small group of 3-5 students, with whom you will meet, prepare and present your analysis to the class. This conference simulates real‑world policy practice and aligns with ASWEAS 2023 standards for authentic, inclusive, and work‑integrated learning.

Group Preparation

Prior to Week 12, your group will meet to integrate key insights from your individual evaluation reports into a concise 5–7 minute presentation. You may use a short slide deck for your presentation. If you decide to use a PowerPoint, please make sure it is no more than 5 slides.

As part of your group preparation, your team will choose the policy audience you wish to present to. This may include, for example, the Australian Parliament, a Department of the Federal Government, a Child, Youth and Family peak body, or any other relevant groups of policy stakeholders. Selecting an audience will help you tailor your communication style, argumentation, and recommendations to reflect real‑world policy practice.

The group presentation should clearly communicate:

·      the policy/program problem

·      key findings from your individual analyses

·      contested concepts or principles shaping the issue

·      impacts on affected groups

·      evidence‑informed recommendations

 Live Presentation

During the conference, your group will deliver the presentation and participate in a brief Q&A session.

You do not have to have finalised your (individual) written program evaluation report to participate in the Social Policy Conference.

 After the conference, you will revise your individual Policy/Program Evaluation Report by incorporating feedback and insights received during the presentation and submit it by the final submission deadline.

learning_activities.topic:
W1: Welcome and what is Australian Social Policy?

Part 1: Introduction to the Course. In the first part of the lecture, we will introduce you to the teaching team and review the aims and objectives of SWSP1013. We will also provide a brief overview of the course structure and assessment. This part of the lecture will be delivered via a 'live Zoom' session. Please see the Blackboard for the Zoom link.


Part 2: The remaining content for Week 1 will be delivered via interactive workbooks (see Blackboard). We begin to explore what is meant by "social policy".


Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_title: W1: Welcome and what is Australian Social Policy?
learning_activities.topic_description: W1: Welcome and what is Australian Social Policy? Part 1: Introduction to the Course. In the first part of the lecture, we will introduce you to the teaching team and review the aims and objectives of SWSP1013. We will also provide a brief overview of the course structure and assessment. This part of the lecture will be delivered via a 'live Zoom' session. Please see the Blackboard for the Zoom link. Part 2: The remaining content for Week 1 will be delivered via interactive workbooks (see Blackboard). We begin to explore what is meant by "social policy".

learning_activities.topic:
W2: Understanding social policy 'problems'

Social policy influences the lives of individual Australians and the nature of our society. It affects whether we can get a job, obtain housing and health care, pay for our everyday needs and have support when needed (McClelland et al., 2021, p.xi). In this lecture, we will explore and critique a number of approaches to social policy and social problems. We also consider how different political values shape how social problems are understood and the policy responses used to address them.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: W2: Understanding social policy 'problems' Social policy influences the lives of individual Australians and the nature of our society. It affects whether we can get a job, obtain housing and health care, pay for our everyday needs and have support when needed (McClelland et al., 2021, p.xi). In this lecture, we will explore and critique a number of approaches to social policy and social problems. We also consider how different political values shape how social problems are understood and the policy responses used to address them.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.topic:
W3: Addressing problems - policy design principles

Different values and beliefs "influence how the ends (goals) and means of social policy are understood and the priority accorded to some ends over others. These in turn can influence the design principles of welfare" (McClelland, 2014, p.20). In this lecture we will explore how social policy attempts to address policy 'problems' by looking at key policy design principles and concepts.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: W3: Addressing problems - policy design principles Different values and beliefs "influence how the ends (goals) and means of social policy are understood and the priority accorded to some ends over others. These in turn can influence the design principles of welfare" (McClelland, 2014, p.20). In this lecture we will explore how social policy attempts to address policy 'problems' by looking at key policy design principles and concepts.

learning_activities.topic:
W4: What's the problem represented to be?

In this lecture, we explore how social policies define and categorise social problems.

We will be introducing Bacchi's "What's the Problem Represented to be (WPR)?" approach to policy analysis.

(This is particularly relevant for assessment 2!)

We'll also have time during the online Q&A session and the tutorials for questions about your in-class exam (scheduled in week 5)

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.topic:
W6: Health Policy

McClelland et al (2021:3) argue that "Social policy aims to improve people’s well-being, and is especially concerned with the welfare of those who experience some form of disadvantage". In this lecture, we turn our focus to health policy in Australia. We examine the key debates, institutional arrangements, and policy goals that underpin Australia’s health system. We explore how Medicare, public health frameworks, primary care structures, and private health insurance operate as interconnected components of the national health architecture.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: W6: Health Policy McClelland et al (2021:3) argue that " Social policy aims to improve people’s well-being, and is especially concerned with the welfare of those who experience some form of disadvantage ". In this lecture, we turn our focus to health policy in Australia. We examine the key debates, institutional arrangements, and policy goals that underpin Australia’s health system. We explore how Medicare, public health frameworks, primary care structures, and private health insurance operate as interconnected components of the national health architecture.

learning_activities.topic:
W7: Social Security

In this lecture, we turn our focus to social security policy in Australia. We examine the institutional arrangements, policy goals, and key debates that shape the design and delivery of social security across sectors in Australia. This includes analysing how social security systems have evolved over time, the ideological assumptions underpinning welfare provision, and the tensions between protection, conditionality, and activation. Particular attention is given to how these policy settings affect structurally disadvantaged groups, including refugee-background and asylum-seeking people, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples, older people and young people and other socially disadvantaged groups.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: W7: Social Security In this lecture, we turn our focus to social security policy in Australia. We examine the institutional arrangements, policy goals, and key debates that shape the design and delivery of social security across sectors in Australia. This includes analysing how social security systems have evolved over time, the ideological assumptions underpinning welfare provision, and the tensions between protection, conditionality, and activation. Particular attention is given to how these policy settings affect structurally disadvantaged groups, including refugee-background and asylum-seeking people, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples, older people and young people and other socially disadvantaged groups.

learning_activities.topic:
W8: Education Policy (& questions about assessment 2)

Debates about equity, social justice, equality and redistribution shape the design of social policy. In this lecture, we turn our focus to education policy in Australia. We examine the historical developments, key debates, institutional arrangements, and policy goals that shape the contemporary education system. This includes analysing how schooling, tertiary education, funding models, curriculum frameworks, and accountability mechanisms operate as interconnected components of Australia’s education architecture. We also consider how these arrangements reproduce or challenge social inequalities, particularly for structurally disadvantaged groups such as refugee-background and asylum-seeking young people, whose educational pathways are shaped by settlement policies, language support systems, and broader political narratives about inclusion, citizenship, and national identity.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

We also consider how these arrangements reproduce or challenge social inequalities, particularly for structurally disadvantaged groups such as refugee-background and asylum-seeking young people, whose educational pathways are shaped by settlement policies, language support systems, and broader political narratives about inclusion, citizenship, and national identity.

learning_activities.topic:
W9: Population & Immigration Policy

 In Week 9, we turn our focus to Australia’s population and immigration policy landscape. We examine key historical developments, major ideological shifts, and the central policy debates that have shaped Australia’s approach to migration over time. Particular attention is given to the structural and policy-level issues affecting refugee-background and asylum-seeking people, including visa regimes, work rights, access to services, detention practices, and the broader political narratives that influence belonging, inclusion, and national identity.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: W9: Population & Immigration Policy In Week 9, we turn our focus to Australia’s population and immigration policy landscape. We examine key historical developments, major ideological shifts, and the central policy debates that have shaped Australia’s approach to migration over time. Particular attention is given to the structural and policy-level issues affecting refugee-background and asylum-seeking people, including visa regimes, work rights, access to services, detention practices, and the broader political narratives that influence belonging, inclusion, and national identity.

learning_activities.topic:
W10: Un/Employment

In this lecture, we examine the institutional arrangements, policy goals, and key debates shaping employment and unemployment policy in Australia. We explore how un/employment is defined and understood in Australia, as well as how labour market regulation, welfare-to-work frameworks, and income support systems have evolved over time. We also examine the ideological assumptions and structural arrangements affecting access to fair work.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: W10: Un/Employment In this lecture, we examine the institutional arrangements, policy goals, and key debates shaping employment and unemployment policy in Australia. We explore how un/employment is defined and understood in Australia, as well as how labour market regulation, welfare-to-work frameworks, and income support systems have evolved over time. We also examine the ideological assumptions and structural arrangements affecting access to fair work.

W10: Un/Employment

learning_activities.topic:
W11 Environmental Policy

This week, we introduce environmental policy through the lens of EcoSocial Work, a framework that links social work and environmental policy. We explore how climate change, pollution, and resource extraction produce disproportionate environmental harms for First Nations, low‑income, and other marginalised communities. A central focus is how equity, diversity, and inclusion can be integrated into environmental justice policy frameworks, paying attention to both the structural causes of disadvantage and the practices that can make policy more just and inclusive.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

We explore how climate change, pollution, and resource extraction produce disproportionate environmental harms for First Nations, low‑income, and other marginalised communities. A central focus is how equity, diversity, and inclusion can be integrated into environmental justice policy frameworks, paying attention to both the structural causes of disadvantage and the practices that can make policy more just and inclusive.

learning_activities.topic:
W12: Housing & Homelessness

In this lecture, we examine the institutional arrangements, policy goals, and key debates underpinning housing and homelessness policy in Australia. We outline the governance structures and funding mechanisms that shape access to safe, secure, and affordable housing and the prevention of homelessness, and critically engage with contested questions regarding the definition of housing need, the adequacy of interventions, and the extent to which policy responses address the structural drivers of housing insecurity and disadvantage.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

We outline the governance structures and funding mechanisms that shape access to safe, secure, and affordable housing and the prevention of homelessness, and critically engage with contested questions regarding the definition of housing need, the adequacy of interventions, and the extent to which policy responses address the structural drivers of housing insecurity and disadvantage.

learning_activities.topic:
Social Policy Conferencing

In the first half of this tutorial, there will be a few activities and Q&A questions regarding the weekly course content. In the second half of the tutorial, we shall begin the Social Policy Conference. Groups will present their program evaluation to the class. After the presentation, you will have the opportunity to incorporate feedback/insights from the conference into your written/individual work and submit by the deadline available on the Blackboard site (during the Examination period).

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
